title: Todd Haynes on Growing Up and Going ‘Wonderstruck’ | The Big Picture (Ep. 30)
format: Todd Haynes on directing kids, casting a deaf actor to lead a silent film, and why he keeps circling back to Hitchcock.

films.title: Wonderstruck
films.note: His new film, adapted from Brian Selznick's novel: two deaf kids, one in the 1920s and one in the 1970s, both searching New York for a lost parent.

films.title: Hugo
films.note: Scorsese's earlier Selznick adaptation — the reason costume designer Sandy Powell thought of Haynes for this one.

films.title: Carol
films.note: His favorite of his own films, and the one he says has since built a real fan cult.

films.title: Dottie Gets Spanked
films.note: His 1993 short, told from a seven-year-old's point of view — the closest he'd come to a kids' film before this one.

films.title: Romeo and Juliet
films.note: The Zeffirelli version. Haynes saw it at seven, a bit young for it, and made his own Super 8 version at nine, playing multiple parts.

films.title: Oliver!
films.note: The one his friends all saw first, before some of them graduated to Romeo and Juliet.

films.title: The Miracle Worker
films.note: The Helen Keller story he got into at 12 — the same age as the kids in Wonderstruck.

films.title: Poison
films.note: His first feature, drawing on Jean Genet.

films.title: Velvet Goldmine
films.note: His glam-rock film, which he calls a tribute to fandom's own creativity.

films.title: Mildred Pierce
films.note: The Michael Curtiz/Joan Crawford version — Haynes read James M. Cain's novel and found it different enough to adapt himself.

films.title: Pete's Dragon
films.note: The film that led Haynes to his Ben, the hearing kid cast to play a boy who goes deaf.

films.title: Vertigo
films.note: Still his No. 1 Hitchcock, though he says it's close.

films.title: Sabotage
films.note: A Hitchcock he'd underrated until revisiting it.

films.title: Dial M for Murder
films.note: Another one he says he'd wrongly written off as minor Hitchcock.
